text: Established in 1863, the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) has long been regarded as the gold standard of humanitarian intervention. However, despite a legacy spanning more than 150 years, a growing chorus of former officials suggests the organization is currently in decline, fueled by a series of contentious policy shifts. Critics argue that the ICRC has compromised its hallmark neutrality and independence while expanding at a rate that has diluted its operational efficiency, trading its reputation for swift, focused fieldwork for bureaucratic overreach. In this analysis, David P. Forsythe re-examines the ICRC’s strategic decisions over recent decades. He contends that while the organization faces significant challenges and has committed notable errors, talk of its fatal decline is exaggerated. Forsythe suggests that while marginal policy corrections are necessary to restore organizational focus, the ICRC remains a vital global actor whose demise has been predicted prematurely.
